perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be effective as of the Effective Date and may be terminated by either party upon thirty (30) day's prior written notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be effective as of the Effective Date and may be terminated by either party upon fourteen (14) day's prior written notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period from 30 days to 14 days may conflict with New Jersey law, which may imply a longer reasonable notice period depending on the circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey law regarding reasonable notice for contract termination
perturbation.law_explanation: N.J.S.A. 12A:2-309 states that where a contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. If the agreed-upon period of 14 days is not 'reasonable', it contradicts this statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 30 to 14 days. The law explanation indicates this may contradict N.J.S.A. 12A:2-309 if the 14-day period is not considered 'reasonable.' Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically N.J. Rev. Stat. § 12A:2-309 (3), states that termination requires 'reasonable notification,' and an agreement dispensing with reasonable notification is invalid if unconscionable. Since the changed text reduces the notification period, it introduces an inconsistency that could render the contract unenforceable if the 14-day notice is deemed unreasonable under the law, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the law of the United States of America and the State of New Jersey without regard to conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the law of the State of Delaware without regard to conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware may conflict with New Jersey law if the parties are primarily located and operating in New Jersey, potentially impacting the enforceability of certain provisions under New Jersey statutes, particularly those protecting employees or consumers.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey Choice of Law Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: New Jersey courts will honor the parties’ agreement as to which law will govern their agreement so long as: (1) the state selected has a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction, or (2) there is any other reasonable basis for the parties’ choice. If neither of these exists and if New Jersey has a materially greater interest in the litigation than the chosen state, then New Jersey law should apply despite the parties' agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies New Jersey law, while the changed text specifies Delaware law. The Law Explanation states that New Jersey courts will honor the parties’ agreement as to which law will govern their agreement so long as: (1) the state selected has a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction, or (2) there is any other reasonable basis for the parties’ choice. The Perturbation Explanation states that changing the governing law to Delaware may conflict with New Jersey law if the parties are primarily located and operating in New Jersey. Based on the Perturbation and Law explanations, there is a legal contradiction because choosing Delaware law may not be honored by New Jersey courts if Delaware does not have a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction. The change could thus invalidate portions of the contract. This falls under Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction, where a contract sets a requirement that violates federal or state law.

perturbation.original_text: Upon completion of the aforesaid evaluation and in the absence of further agreement of the parties, the Recipient shall cease all use and make no further use of the Information. At Disclosing Party's request, the Recipient shall promptly return or destroy all Information disclosed by Disclosing Party and shall retain no copies.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon completion of the aforesaid evaluation and in the absence of further agreement of the parties, the Recipient shall cease all use and make no further use of the Information. The Recipient is not obligated to return or destroy any documents.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to return or destroy confidential information after evaluation may violate standard trade secret protection principles under New Jersey law, especially if the recipient retains the information and uses it against the disclosing party.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the requirement to return or destroy confidential information is against the best practices for protecting confidential information and trade secrets, where reasonable measures are needed to avoid misappropriation. The recipient should no longer have access to the confidential information once the purpose is served. If they retain the information and use it against the disclosing party, this could be viewed as a violation of trade secret misappropriation laws.

Appropriation of name, brand, trade-mark, reputation or goodwill of maker by dealer in product of maker No merchant, firm or corporation shall appropriate for his or their own use a name, brand, trade-mark, reputation or goodwill of any maker in whose product such merchant, firm or corporation deals. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the Recipient's obligation to return or destroy confidential information, directly contradicting the principle that reasonable measures are needed to avoid misappropriation of trade secrets. The Law Explanation states that retaining information after the purpose is served could lead to trade secret misappropriation, implying a violation of laws protecting confidential information. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses appropriation of brand names, trademarks, etc., which is somewhat related to unfair trade practices, the Perturbation Explanation highlights that the removal of the return/destroy obligation violates standard trade secret protection principles under New Jersey law. This, coupled with the Law Explanation and the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction being 'when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal' supports the determination that the changed text creates a legal contradiction.
